title: Pressure-cooker beef stew
subtitle: Chuck steak braised in stock and red wine under pressure for 35 minutes.
excerpt: Chuck steak browned and then braised under pressure with onion, carrot, mushrooms, and red wine. The pressure cooker pulls in 35 minutes what a slow oven does in three hours. Same depth of flavour, weeknight time scale.
sourceNotes: Pressure-braised beef stew uses the same fundamentals as a slow-oven version (Larousse Gastronomique, Elizabeth David's French Provincial Cooking): brown the meat, deglaze with wine, cook with aromatics until the collagen yields. The pressure cooker simply accelerates the collagen-to-gelatin conversion that takes hours in an open pot. The browning step still happens at atmospheric pressure in the cooker's saute mode; the high-pressure phase does the tenderising.

content.text: Chuck steak is the right cut for braising. It has the collagen-rich connective tissue that breaks down into gelatin, giving the stew its glossy, mouth-coating body. Lean cuts dry out under pressure; chuck rewards it.

The cooker does two jobs. First, sauté mode at atmospheric pressure: brown the meat, sweat the aromatics, deglaze the pan. Then the lid goes on for the 35-minute high-pressure phase that drives the actual tenderising. Skipping the browning step costs you the flavour foundation; nothing the pressure does later replaces it.

items.name: Beef chuck
items.prepNote: cut into 4 cm cubes, patted dry

content.text: A 6-litre pressure cooker with a sauté function. A stovetop pressure cooker works the same way; brown the meat in a separate frying pan first if your cooker base scorches easily.

Pat {{beef-chuck}} of beef cubes dry with kitchen paper. Toss them with {{plain-flour}} of flour and a generous pinch of salt and pepper. Set the pressure cooker to sauté or high heat and warm {{vegetable-oil}} of oil. Brown the beef in two batches: do not crowd the pan or the meat steams rather than colours. Each batch needs four minutes a side. Transfer to a plate.

Add {{onion}} chopped onions, {{carrot}} carrot chunks, and {{celery}} celery to the cooker. Cook for 5 minutes until the onions soften. Stir in {{mushrooms-chestnut}} of quartered mushrooms and cook for 3 minutes more. Add {{garlic}} of sliced garlic and {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée; cook for one minute. Pour in {{red-wine}} of red wine and scrape the base of the pan to release any browned bits.

Return the beef and any resting juices to the cooker. Add {{stock-beef}} of hot beef stock, {{bay-leaves}} of bay, and {{thyme}} of thyme. The liquid should come about three-quarters of the way up the meat; it does not need to cover it. Lock the lid and cook at high pressure for 35 minutes. Let the pressure release naturally for 15 minutes, then quick-release any remaining pressure.

Open the lid carefully. If the sauce is thin, set the cooker back to sauté and reduce uncovered for 5 minutes. Taste and adjust the salt. Fish out the bay leaves and thyme stems. Serve over mashed potato, polenta, or with crusty bread to mop the sauce.

items.fix: Reseal and pressure-cook for another 10 minutes, then natural-release. Chuck takes 35 to 45 minutes at high pressure depending on cube size.
items.cause: Cooking time too short for the cut, or quick-released the pressure too soon.

items.fix: Reduce uncovered on sauté mode for 5 to 10 minutes. Next time, cut the stock to 300 ml.
items.cause: Too much liquid for the cooker's evaporation rate, which is near-zero under pressure.

items.fix: Add 100 ml of hot stock and stir. The cooker needs liquid to generate steam.
items.cause: Sauce too thick or not enough liquid for steam.

items.fix: Always deglaze thoroughly with wine and a wooden spoon before adding the stock. If burnt, lift the meat into a clean pot and transfer the sauce through a sieve.
items.cause: Tomato purée or flour stuck to the base after deglazing.

items.fix: Reduce on sauté for 10 minutes after pressure-cooking. The flour cooks out and the sauce thickens at the same time.
items.cause: Flour did not cook out during browning.

content.text: The stew tastes better the next day; the flavours marry as it sits. Refrigerate for up to four days. Freeze for up to three months in single portions; defrost overnight in the fridge and reheat gently.

British home cooking has carried beef stew through every generation, usually as a low oven braise. The pressure cooker brings the long-simmered version within weeknight reach, without losing the depth that long cooking gives. The method is universal. Any tough beef cut, with any aromatic-and-wine combination, follows the same shape.
